titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Luc-Daniel Dupire Collection AD.MC.089
titlestmt.author: Brad Bauer

publicationstmt.publisher: NYU Abu Dhabi, Archives and Special Collections

address.addressline: New York University Abu Dhabi, C-2

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-04-06 14:23:25 +0400.
profiledesc.langusage: Description is written in: English, Latin script.

repository.corpname: NYU Abu Dhabi, Archives and Special Collections

did.unittitle: Luc-Daniel Dupire collection

physdesc.extent: in 3 document boxes and one flat box.

did.abstract: Reports, brochures, broadsides, booklets, audio recording, and other ephemeral materials collected by Belgian political activist Luc-Daniel Dupire, documenting Marxist political movements in Oman and the Gulf region during the 1970s and 1980s, including the armed rebellion in the Dhofar region of Oman.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions governing access
accessrestrict.p: The Luc-Daniel Dupire Collection is open and available for use in the Archives and Special Collections department of the New York University Abu Dhabi Library.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The Luc-Daniel Dupire collection is grouped into three series: I. PFLOAG, II. Other groups, III. Books, photographs, and sound recordings. Each group is arranged in alphabetical order by folder title or the names of constituent groups.

bioghist.head: Biographical note
bioghist.p: Luc-Daniel Dupire, born 1942, was a leftist political activist based in Brussels during the 1960s and 1970s. As a university student at the Free University of Brussels (ULB) in the early 1960s, he became acquainted with several Congolese students, and through them, involved with groups such as the General Union of Congolese Students (UGEC). Dupire's acquaintance with these students led him to travel to Leopoldville (later known as Kinshasa) in August 1963 to attend the second general congress of the UGEC, where he expanded his network with further activists there. He also played a role in the formation of another organization based in Belgium, the Front des Patriotes Congolais (FPC), or Congolese Patriotic Front.

At the same time, he was active in the Communist Party of Belgium (PCB), and in particular in that group's Maoist wing led by Jacques Grippa. It was through his connections there that he was introduced to a Congolese student based in West Germany, Kalixte Mukendi Wa Nsanga, who advocated for a militaristic approach to revolution in Congo, as opposed to the views of many of the students in the UGEC, who wanted to use the education they gained at universities in Europe to contribute to the development on an independent Congo. Dupire and Wa Nsanga traveled to Algiers in January 1964 and met with other Congolese students, including those who had been expelled from Belgium due to their activism, and through this meeting they formed a new, more radical organization called the Union des Jeunesses Révolutionnaires Congolaises (UJRC), or Union of Congolese Revolutionary Youth. Dupire, who adopted the pseudonym of "Musenga-Banza," served on that group's political bureau and also as co-editor of its publication, "L'Eclair." While active in the UJRC, he also contributed to the journal of the PCB, "La Voix du Peuple," serving as an African correspondent and later as that publication's editor-in-chief. With his dual roles in both organizations, he also accompanied delegations to Rome and Tirana in 1967 to discuss the possible formation of a Congolese Communist Party.

In the late 1960s, the violent excesses of the Chinese Cultural Revolution led to a split in the PCB, between those who supported the policies of Mao Zedong and those who supported Liu Shaoqi, and at this moment, Dupire left the party. In 1969 he formed the "Comité Palestine de Bruxelles," and then from 1970-1976 led a related organization, the "Comité National Palestine de Belgique." All of the material in this present collection dates from Dupire's activities in these latter organizations. It was during the 1970s and into the early 1980s that Dupire became more active in mainstream Belgian politics, serving in the cabinets of governments led by the Francophone socialist party, the Parti Socialiste (1972-1973, 1977-1982). From 1977-1979, Dupire was involved in a non-aligned political movement, the "Mouvement Belge pour l'Indépendance (Européenne) Face aux Deux Superpuissances" (MB/MBI), an organization that sought a middle-way between movements dominated by the United States and the Soviet Union.

In the 1980s, Dupire shifted his focus to activism around gay and lesbian rights in Belgium, hosting a public radio program in 1981, "Antenne-Rose," which later resulted in a weekly publication, "Tels-Quels." "Antenne-Rose" became an officially recognized non-profit organization in 1984, with Dupire assuming the role of secretary, and later as chair (1986-1987).
Site on AbeBooks.UK, of vendor "LivresCollector," selling several titles related to Dupire, see: https://www.abebooks.co.uk/livrescollector-bruxelles/55841777/sf [Accessed April 2026]
Monaville, Pedro. "Students of the World: Global 1968 and Decolonization in the Congo," (Duke Univ. Press, 2022). See in particular Chapter 7, pp. 145-159.

prefercite.head: Preferred citation
prefercite.p: [item], [folder title], [box/folder number], Luc-Daniel Dupire collection, MC-089, New York University Abu Dhabi Library, Abu Dhabi, UAE.

relatedmaterial.head: Related materials
relatedmaterial.p: Another related collection exists at the Université Libre de Bruxelles (Free University of Brussels), under Fond Z 094, "Archives du Comité National Palestine." The online finding aid for this collection can be found at: https://catalogue.archives.ulb.be/index.php/archives-du-comite-national-palestine .

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: The Luc-Daniel Dupire collection consists of reports, brochures, broadsides, booklets, audio recording, and other ephemeral materials collected by Belgian political activist Luc-Daniel Dupire, documenting Marxist political movements in Oman and the Gulf region during the 1970s and 1980s, including the armed rebellion in the Dhofar region of Oman. Most of the material appears to have been collected as part of Dupire's activities as a key member of the Comité National Palestine de Belgique, based in Brussels.

The bulk of the materials in the collection originated from either the Popular Front for the Liberation of Oman and the Arabian Gulf (PFLOAG), or from allied organizations outside of the Middle East. While the PFLOAG had existed since the mid-1960s under a slightly different name (Popular Front for the Liberation of the Occupied Arabian Gulf), most of the materials in this collection date from its iteration from 1973 onward, including a few scattered materials after 1974, when the group was renamed the Popular Front for the Liberation of Oman (PFLO), and shortly before its demise following the end of the Dhofar Rebellion in 1976. In each of these phases, the group was marked by its emphasis on Marxist-Leninist ideology and its focus on armed combat with both local rulers as well as British interests in the region. The group was most active during the uprising in the southern Omani region of Dhofar between the late 1960s and the mid 1970s, and was supported by the Marxist regime of the neighboring country of South Yemen (People's Democratic Republic of Yemen), as well by leftist groups based overseas who were sympathetic to its cause.

The first group of materials in the collection, grouped under the series "PFLOAG," is divided into subseries of documents and serial publications. The documents include communiques issued by the group, from offices in Aden, Algiers, and Brussels; broadsides, and materials related to two conferences that brought together foreign solidarity groups, the first taking place in Aden in December 1973, and the second in Paris in December 1974. In addition, a press kit for the documentary by Lebanese filmmaker Heiny Srour, "L'Heure de la Libération a sonne," is included, as are some scattered documents from the PFLO, after 1974. The publication subseries includes a group of 28 scattered issues of the PFLOAG newsletter, "Sawt al-Thawrah" ("Voice of the Revolution"), as well as the later PFLO publication, "'Uman al-Thawrah," and a couple dozen pamphlets on various political themes, printed in Aden or Beirut.

The second group of materials consists chiefly of newsletters from some of the "solidarity" groups from abroad, including the Gulf Committee (London), the Comité de Soutien à la Révolution d'Oman (Paris and Toulouse), and the Comité National Palestine de Belgique (Belgium), the latter of which includes handwritten notes, likely by Dupire, of an interview with British activist and scholar Fred Halliday. Also included are materials from allied groups in the United States, West Germany, the Netherlands, and Denmark, and elsewhere. As such, this group provides a glimpse at the activities and networks of Marxist-influenced political organizations in Western Europe and the United States in the 1970s, and how they viewed the rebellion in Dhofar as part of a larger, international revolutionary movement in the global south.

The last group of materials include books, photographs, and one sound recording. The photographs comprise the largest number of materials in this series, and consist of photographic prints depicting chiefly the activities of the PFLOAG in the mountains of Dhofar, including soldiers, school classrooms, and activities of women, as well as belongings captured from killed Iranian soldiers. Also included are general images of rulers, chiefly of Sultan Qaboos of Oman meeting with rulers such as Gerald Ford (U.S.), Valery Giscard d'Estaing (France), King Hussein of Jordan, and Shah Mohamed Reza Pahlavi of Iran. Also included is one record album, titled "Chants Révolutionnaires d'Oman, du Yémen et d'Iran."

userestrict.head: Conditions governing use
userestrict.p: Unpublished as well as published content within the collection may be protected by copyright, and any uses of such material beyond what is commonly accepted as "fair use" require the express written permission of the copyright holder.

controlaccess.subject: Protest movements -- Europe, Western -- 20th century
controlaccess.corpname: Jabhah al-Waṭanīyah al-Dīmuqrāṭīyah li-Taḥrīr ʻUmān wa-al-Khalīj al-ʻArabī

did.unittitle: PFLOAG
Documents
Broadside: "Appeal from P.F.L.O." (one sheet)
Broadside: "al-Jabha al-sha'bbiyah li- ṭaḥrīr 'Umān: 'alá ṭarīq al-thawrah al-sha'bbīyah al-musalaḥah"
Communiqué, describing meeting between leaders of P.F.L.O.A.G. and delegation from World Peace Council, in Aden (1 sheet)
Communiqué: "Memorandum to Organisations Concerned with Abused Human Dignity" (3 pages)
Communiqués, Arabic
Communiqués, English
Communiqués, English
Communiqués, English
Communiqués, French (Algiers office)
Communiqués, French (Brussels), manuscript drafts
First Conference of the Solidarity Committees (Aden): documents, including communiqués, texts of speeches, agenda of meetings, issued by the conference
First Conference of the Solidarity Committees (Aden): reports and statements from solidarity groups, in Europe, Africa, North America
Poster, "All Solidarity with the Struggle of the People in Oman and the Arab Gulf," First Conference of the Solidarity Committees (Aden)
Press kit: for the film "L'Heure de la Libération a sonne," directed by Heiny Srour (two booklets)
Second International Conference of Solidarity with the Revolution in Oman (Paris): reports from solidarity committees, requests for medicine shipments, statements
Publications
"Kayf taḥāfaẓ al-imbarīyalīyah 'alá muṣāliha fī al-Khalīj al-'Arabī" (48 pages)
"Mawāqif mu'tamar ḥamrīn al-thawrah wa al-marā'ah" (24 pages)
"Min aḥdāth al-sanat al-sābi'ah lil-thawrah...." (40 + [9] pages)
"Wathā'iq al-mu'tamar al-tā'sīsī..." (32 pages)
"Man yaḥkum 'Umān al-yawm?" (79 pages)
"al-Waḍ' al- ṭabaqī fī 'Umān" (32 pages, Beirut: Dār al-Ṭalīʻah)
al-Ittihạ̄d al-Watạnī Tạlabat ʻUmān. "Dustūr aqar bil-muʼtamar al-taʼsīsī" (24 pages)
"Shahādāt min al-manātịq al-muhạrrarah" (60 pages, [ʻAden] : al-Jabhah al-Shaʻbīyah li-Tahṛīr ʻUmān)
"al-Thawrah al-ʻUmānīyah, 1957-1959" (43 pages, Beirut: Dār al-Ṭalīʻah)
"Wathāʼiq al-niḍāl al-waṭanī, 1965-1974" (Bayrūt : Dār al-Ṭalīʻah)
"al-Ghazw al-īrānī li-ʻUmān fī ʻāmihi al-thānī" (52 pages, Aden: al-Jabhah al-Shaʻbīyah li-Tahṛīr ʻUmān, al-Lajnah al-Iʻlāmīyah)
"The regionalization of the war in Oman and the fate of the tribal groups in Dhofar" (29 pages)
Periodical: Saut al Thawrah, No. 32 [English]
Periodical: Ṣawt al-Thawrah [Arabic], duplicate set, issues 393, 401-402, 407-412, 414, 416-417, 419-422, 424-427
Periodical: ʻUmān al-thawrah [Arabic], scattered issues
Harakat al-taḥrir al-waṭanī wa niḍāl sha'ab 'Umān wa al-Khalīj al-'Arabī (12 pages)
Other groups
Afro-Asian Affairs (newsletter, Afro-Asian Publications, Ltd. : London), No. 2
Arabische Studenten Vereinigung (Aachen): Communique (Arabic)
Arbeitskreis Nahost / Golf: "Politische Gefangene in den Ölstaaten: Bahrein, Iran, Oman, Saudi-Arabien"
Arbeitskreis Nahost / Golf: Broadside and pamphlets
Comité de Soutien à la Révolution d'Oman: documents (broadside, communiques_
Comité de Soutien à la Révolution d'Oman (Paris): "La lutte du peuple en Oman" (28 pages)
Comité de Soutien à la Révolution d'Oman (Toulouse): "Le mouvement de libération nationale et la lutte du peuple d'Oman et du Golfe-Arabique" (booklet)
Comité de Soutien à la Révolution d'Oman (Toulouse): "Deux revolutions inconnue, Yemen Democratique et Oman"
Comités Indochine Palestine: "Oman-Golfe Arabe, La guerre du peuple vaincra" (Dossier Moyen-Orient, No. 1)
Comité National Palestine de Belgique (Brussels): "Documents concernant les prisonniers politiques en Oman"
Comité National Palestine de Belgique (Brussels): handwritten notes from an interview with Fred Halliday
Comité National Palestine de Belgique (Brussels): petitions, signed (photocopies), protesting the treatment of prisoners in Oman
Front National de Libération du Sud-Yémen: "Documents sur la Revolution au Sud-Yémenite et la Guerilla du Dhofar," (booklet of copied documents, stapled and bound)
Gulf Committee (London): Communiqué, photocopied, in Arabic
Gulf Committee (London): "Documents of the National Struggle in Oman and the Arabian Gulf"
Gulf Committee (London): "Oil and Investment in Oman" (13 pages)
Gulf Committee (London): "Oman: a class analysis" (20 pages)
Gulf Committee (London): "The Oman War, 1957-1959, a critical history" (24 pages)
Gulf Committee (London): "Political Prisoners in the Oil States; Bahrain, Iran, Oman, Saudi Arabia" (32 pages)
al-Jābhah al-Shaʻbīyah fī al-Baḥrayn (Popular Front in Bahrain): statements (Arabic)
Komiteen for Revolutionen in Oman og den Arabiske Golf (Nyborg, Denmark): documents and brochures
Komiteen for Revolutionen in Oman og den Arabiske Golf (Nyborg, Denmark): "Politiske Fanger i Oliestaterne; Oman, Bahrain, Saudi Arabien, Iran" (40 pages)
Kunfidrālyat Ṭalabat ʿUmān wal-Khalīj al-ʿarabī fī Lubnān: "Kuntu fī Ẓufār : Mushāhadāt fī Arḍ aṯ-ṯhawrah," by al-Jināḥī, Saʿīd Aḥmed.
Munaẓẓamat al-Marʼah al-ʻUmānīyah: Al Marʼah al-ʻUmānīyah, issues 9-10
Nederlands Palestina Komitee (Amsterdam): "Oman, Volk en Verzet, 1965-1975" (54 pages)
Omani Red Crescent = al-Hilāl al-āhmar al-'Umānī (Aden, South Yemen) : documents
Organisation of Yemeni Revolutionary Resisters: communiqué
Organisation of Yemeni Revolutionary Resisters: documents and communiques, Arabic, in folder with group's logo
Palæstinakomiteen og Golfkomiteen (Copenhagen): Imperialismen i Mellemøsten : De arabiske folks kamp mod imperialismen, zionismen og de arabiske reaktionære
Unité de Distribution (Brussels): materials related to screening of the film "L'Heure de la Liberation a Sonne"
Various groups (United Kingdom, Belgium, Italy, Algeria): brochures, flyers, broadsides
Books, photographs and sound recordings
Book: Dardaud, Gabriel; Lacouture, Simonne et Jean: "Les émirats mirages : voyage chez les pétrocrates" (Paris: Éditions du Seuil)
Book: Willemart, Hélène et Pierre. "Dossier du Moyen-Orient arabe. Géographie, économie, politique" (Verviers : Gerard)
Sultan Qaboos with world leaders (4 images, black and white): Gerald Ford, Valery Giscard d'Estaing, King Hussein of Jordan, Shah Reza Mohamed Pahlavi of Iran (large format copy prints)
PFLOAG images (3): depicting fighters with weapons, training camps and schools, Belgian-made weapon, color prints, all from Dhofar
PFLOAG images (10, black and white): guerrilla fighters in Dhofar
PFLOAG images (9, black and white): base camp, villagers, in Dhofar
PFLOAG images (11, black and white): aftermath of battles (dead soldiers, identification cards, wreckage), in Dhofar
PFLOAG images (2, black and white): protesters in Europe, construction of the palace of Sultan Qaboos in Muscat
Sound recording: Chants Révolutionnaires d'Oman, du Yémen et d'Iran. 1 disc, 33.3 rpm (Paris: Expression Spontanér)
